text: The right side of the heart is approached first.
The pericardium is opened, revealing the right atrium.
The heart is easily seen to be in atrial fibrillation with an irregular heartbeat and a twitching or fibrillating atrium seen.
With retraction sutures in place, the superior vena cava, or SVC, and pulmonary veins, PV, can be identified.
Rapid pacing helps identify nerve plexi that can be triggers for atrial fibrillation.
A bipolar clamp using radiofrequency or RF energy creates a circumferential tissue ablation around the right pulmonary veins.
This isolates any triggers of atrial fibrillation from the remainder of the heart.
Repeat pacing and sensing with a pen-like device ensures complete isolation of the pulmonary veins on the right side.
Connecting lesions are now performed across the back of the left atrium.
This allows a box lesion set to be completed from the left side.
In this particular patient, normal sinus rhythm has been achieved with just the partial box set and ablation.
The pericardium is again opened, and retraction sutures help reveal the pulmonary veins and left atrial appendage.
A lighted dissecting device ensures safe encircling of the pulmonary veins, as was done on the right side.
The bipolar clamp is in a position for ablation of the pulmonary veins.
Additional sensing and pacing confirms complete isolation of the left pulmonary veins.
The left atrial appendage is then removed with a stapling device as 90% of strokes occur from thrombus originating in this structure.
This is what the appendage looks like after re-removal.
